evidence_quotes.quote: When a company abandons a game or online servicemeaning it shuts down essential servers, disables DRM activation, or removes access so that paying customers can no longer reasonably play what they bought for at least twelve monthsthat company should be deemed to have permanently forfeited its exclusive rights to that product.
The preserved or community-maintained version should reflect the final, working build that consumers actually experienced before shutdown.
Under use-it-or-lose-it, the rule is self-executing: maintain a minimal playable build and keep your rights; abandon it and the public may preserve it.

paragraphs.text: I support the creation of a permanent use-it-or-lose-it rule within the Digital Fairness Act to protect consumers, small developers, and Europes digital cultural heritage. When a company abandons a game or online servicemeaning it shuts down essential servers, disables DRM activation, or removes access so that paying customers can no longer reasonably play what they bought for at least twelve monthsthat company should be deemed to have permanently forfeited its exclusive rights to that product. Once you make a game unplayable, you have voluntarily left it behind, and you should not retain legal power to stop the community from preserving or reviving it. Ownership ends when stewardship ends. This forfeiture must apply to the latest functional state of the product, or as close to it as technically possible. Companies must not be allowed to evade responsibility by releasing a crippled or token compliance build that barely runs but satisfies paperwork. The preserved or community-maintained version should reflect the final, working build that consumers actually experienced before shutdown. Likewise, the rule must permanently eliminate token reactivation loopholes whereby a company briefly relaunches or patches a title to reset a regulatory clock and maintain monopoly control. Under a true use-it-or-lose-it model, exclusive rights cannot be revived later; the community remains free to operate servers, patch clients, and maintain access for players who already paid. Crucially, the solution must avoid fines, license fees, and regulatory costs that only large publishers can absorb. Any fixed penaltywhether 50 000 or 500 000 simply strengthens monopolies and destroys smaller creators who cannot afford compliance lawyers or perpetual maintenance. Requiring ongoing updates or lifelong support likewise turns passion projects into liabilities. The goal is fairness through deregulation, not new financial hurdles. Under use-it-or-lose-it, the rule is self-executing: maintain a minimal playable build and keep your rights; abandon it and the public may preserve it. No fees, no bureaucracy, no distortion of competition. This approach protects independent studioswho generally welcome preservationwhile removing the leverage that large corporations use to suppress modders, archivists, historians, and community servers. It guarantees that works remain accessible in their authentic form and that the market rewards stewardship rather than deletion. In economic terms, it restores value consumers already purchased and prevents digital destruction of property. In cultural terms, it safeguards Europes creative heritage for study and enjoyment by future generations. In political terms, it is proportionate and clear: if a business wants to keep control, it can simply keep the game playable; if it abandons the product for twelve months, exclusivity ends permanently. I therefore urge the Commission to include a Permanent Use-It-or-Lose-It Abandonment Forfeiture Clause within the Digital Fairness legislative package. This is a fair, efficient, non-bureaucratic solution that defends consumers, protects creators, and preserves culturefully aligned with the goals of a fair, open, and sustainable digital single market.
